perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise mutually agreed in writing, the Receiving Party's obligations with respect to each item of Confidential Information shall terminate five (5) years from the date of the receipt thereof by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise mutually agreed in writing, the Receiving Party's obligations with respect to each item of Confidential Information shall terminate one (1) year from the date of the receipt thereof by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Illinois law, which recognizes that the appropriate duration of confidentiality depends on the nature of the information and the industry. A one-year term may not provide adequate protection for certain types of sensitive business information, potentially conflicting with the requirements for reasonable protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Trade Secrets Act, 765 ILCS 1065/1 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text reduces the term of confidentiality to one year. Under the Illinois Trade Secrets Act, trade secrets must be subject to efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Depending on the nature of the trade secret, a one-year confidentiality period may not be considered a reasonable effort to maintain secrecy, especially for long-term proprietary information, thereby undermining the protections afforded by the Act. 765 ILCS 1065/8 allows for attorney's fees to be awarded if a claim of misappropriation is made in bad faith. While this section does not directly state the length of time needed to protect trade secrets, it does reinforce that companies must take reasonable actions to protect trade secrets and could be construed that one year is not enough.

(Source: Laws 1965, p. 577.) 805 ILCS 35/5 (805 ILCS 35/5) (from Ch. 32, par. 1005) Sec. 5. Any 3 or more qualified natural persons, all of whom shall be bona fide residents of this State, who desire to associate themselves for the purpose of establishing and operating a development credit corporation may subscribe, acknowledge and file with the Director for approval articles of incorporation as set out in Section 11. (Source: Laws 1965, p. 577.) 805 ILCS 35/6 (805 ILCS 35/6) (from Ch. 32, par. 1006) Sec. 6. As soon as practicable after the receipt of the proposed articles of incorporation, the Director shall, from the best sources of information available, ascertain the character and general fitness of the applicants and their standing in their respective communities. The Director shall issue his certificate approving the articles of incorporation and authorizing the applicants to proceed with the organization of the development credit corporation if he is satisfied: (1) That all the applicants are bona fide residents of the State; (2) That they have the confidence of their respective communities; (3) That the proposed articles of incorporation conform to the provisions of Section 11 of this Act. (Source: Laws 1965, p. 577.) 805 ILCS 35/6.1 (805 ILCS 35/6.1) Sec. 6.1. All moneys received by the Department of Financial Institutions under this Act shall be deposited in the Financial Institution Fund created under Section 6z-26 of the State Finance Act. (Source: P.A. 98-463, eff. 8-16-13.) 805 ILCS 35/7 (805 ILCS 35/7) (from Ch. 32, par. 1007) Sec. 7. Upon receipt of the certificate of preliminary approval the applicants may proceed to complete the organization of the development credit corporation, to obtain subscriptions for and payment of its capital stock, and do all other things necessarily incidental and preliminary to its transacting business. (Source: Laws 1965, p. 577.) 805 ILCS 35/8 (805 ILCS 35/8) (from Ch. 32, par. 1008) Sec. 8. When the applicants have completed the organization of the proposed development credit corporation, they shall file with the Director a certificate of organization executed by its president and attested by its secretary and with its seal affixed thereto, certifying: (1) the name and addresses of all of its subscribers of stock, the number of shares subscribed and the number of shares fully paid for by each; (2) the total number of shares subscribed, but not fully paid for; (3) the total number of shares paid in full; (4) the name and address of any depositary holding on deposit any funds of the development credit corporation; (5) the names and addresses of the officers and members of the executive committee, if any, of the development credit corporation. The certificate of organization of the applicant shall be accompanied by: (1) the certificate of any named depositary certifying the amount of funds on deposit to the credit of the development credit corporation; (2) any bylaws or resolutions which have been adopted. (Source: P.A. 91-357, eff. 7-29-99.) 805 ILCS 35/9 (805 ILCS 35/9) (from Ch. 32, par. 1009) Sec. 9. As soon as practicable after the filing of the certificate of organization by the applicants, the Director shall issue to the applicants a certificate of incorporation in such form as he may prescribe, if the Director, from the best information available, determines that: (1) The holders of the fully paid stock of the corporation are at least 10 in number; (2) Not less than $50,000 has been rece
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a five-year confidentiality term, while the changed text reduces it to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation indicate that a one-year term may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Illinois law, as it may not represent a 'reasonable' effort to maintain secrecy, potentially conflicting with the Illinois Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address trade secrets or confidentiality periods, so it cannot be used to confirm or deny the contradiction. Since the shortened term could fail to adequately protect trade secrets as argued in both explanations, and the Illinois Trade Secrets Act expects companies to take reasonable actions to protect trade secrets, this creates a significant legal contradiction by potentially undermining the required protection efforts.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is made subject to, and shall be construed under, the laws of the State of Illinois.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is made subject to, and shall be construed under, the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California could create conflicts if the contract's terms violate California labor laws, such as those related to employee classification or non-compete agreements, given that the contract involves parties in both Illinois and potentially California. This is a conflict because Illinois law is more favorable to non-competes than California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. If this agreement contains provisions that could be interpreted as restricting Receiving Party's ability to compete with MyPoints.com after the termination of the agreement (even implicitly through overly broad confidentiality clauses), it could be deemed unenforceable under California law, whereas it might be enforceable under Illinois law. Switching the governing law to California introduces a potential conflict.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from Illinois to California. The Law Explanation states that California law (Business and Professions Code Section 16600) generally prohibits contracts restraining a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The scraped law snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), confirms this, stating that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The Perturbation Explanation indicates that Illinois law is more favorable to non-competes than California law. Therefore, changing the governing law to California could invalidate non-compete provisions that would have been enforceable under Illinois law, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party agrees to keep the existence and nature of this Agreement confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party agrees to keep the existence and nature of this Agreement confidential indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring indefinite confidentiality regarding the existence and nature of the agreement could run afoul of legal principles discouraging perpetual restraints on information, especially if the information later becomes public or loses its competitive advantage. While some information can be protected indefinitely as a trade secret, the mere existence of an agreement may not warrant such protection. This is a potential issue because the agreement has a fixed termination regarding confidential information in paragraph 5.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Trade Secrets Act defines a trade secret in 765 ILCS 1065/2(d) as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means. Indefinitely restricting the disclosure of the existence and nature of the agreement may not align with the definition of a trade secret if the information does not derive independent economic value from being kept secret or if it becomes generally known. The indefinite restriction may therefore be seen as an unreasonable restraint.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a potential legal contradiction by requiring indefinite confidentiality. The Law Explanation mentions that the Illinois Trade Secrets Act defines a trade secret by whether it derives independent economic value from being not generally known. By extending the confidentiality indefinitely, the agreement risks conflicting with the legal definition of a trade secret if the information doesn't maintain economic value or becomes public, potentially being seen as an unreasonable restraint. While the provided law snippet 1 is unavailable, the Law Explanation suggests a potential conflict with trade secret laws, and the Perturbation Explanation highlights the issue of perpetual restraints and fixed termination clauses, supporting the assessment that a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: In the event MyPoints.com is unable to obtain such protective order or other appropriate remedy, only that portion of the Confidential Information which has been deemed by a written opinion of counsel to be legally required to be furnished, shall be disclosed, and Receiving Party will cooperate with the MyPoints.com to obtain a protective order or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded the Confidential Information so disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event Receiving Party becomes legally compelled to disclose any of the Confidential Information, Receiving Party shall immediately disclose the Confidential Information without notice to MyPoints.com. Receiving Party has no duty to seek a protective order or provide notice to MyPoints.com.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for the Receiving Party to provide prompt notice and seek a protective order before disclosing confidential information, as well as absolving the Receiving Party from any duty to do so, contradicts the principles of good faith and fair dealing implied in contracts under Illinois law. Such a change could be deemed unconscionable as it deprives MyPoints.com of the opportunity to protect its confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois law on implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Illinois Pattern Jury Instructions, Civil, 700.00 Good Faith and Fair Dealing - General Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, Illinois law recognizes an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. This covenant requires parties to act reasonably and honestly in exercising their contractual rights and duties. By explicitly stating that the Receiving Party has no duty to provide notice or seek a protective order, the modified text violates this implied covenant, as it deprives MyPoints.com of the ability to protect its interests and undermines the purpose of the confidentiality agreement. The first URL is to a legal newsroom article about the topic, the second is a journal article.

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly states that the Receiving Party has no duty to provide notice or seek a protective order. This directly contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to act reasonably and honestly in exercising their contractual rights and duties. By removing the obligation to provide notice, the modified text deprives MyPoints.com of the ability to protect its interests and undermines the purpose of the confidentiality agreement. Neither of the provided snippets were useful in determining this answer.
